slides.title: Making Methods Smart 🎓
slides.content:
Our `greetPlayer()` method from the last lesson was a bit dumb. It always said the exact same thing.

What if we want to greet a specific player by their actual name? We need a way to **pass data** into the method when we call it.

We do this using **Parameters**.

slides.title: The Inbox 📥
slides.content:
A Parameter acts like an 'Inbox' for the method. 

We define it inside the parentheses `()` when we create the method. It requires a exact Data Type and a Name.

quizData.question: What happens if we call `greetPlayer();` without putting a name inside the parentheses?

options.text: It asks the user to type their name.

quizData.explanation: If a method demands an input parameter, you MUST provide an argument. Calling it empty causes an error.

slides.title: Multiple Parameters 🗄️🗄️
slides.content:
You can have as many parameters as you want!

Just separate them with commas.

`static void calculateScore(int enemiesDefeated, int timeBonus) { ... }`

slides.title: Multi-Param Action 🎯
slides.content: Here is a method that takes two integers and prints their sum.

quizData.question: If a method is defined as `static void setupPlayer(String name, int age)`, what is wrong with the call `setupPlayer(25, "Bob");`?

options.text: The arguments are in the wrong order.
You can't mix Strings and ints.

quizData.explanation: Java strictly enforces the order. The first parameter is a String, so you must provide a String first.
